moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, immediately staking a claim to the center and controlling the crucial d5 and f5 squares. This classical first move develops our game along traditional principles - we're preparing to bring our pieces into active play while maintaining maximum flexibility for our setup. The e4 advance also opens diagonals for both our light-squared bishop and our queen, giving us multiple developmental options. Our immediate plans include rapid piece development with moves like Nf3, Bc4, and castling kingside to get our king to safety.

Our opponent has chosen the St. George Defense, one of the most unconventional responses to e4 that completely ignores central control in favor of preparing immediate queenside expansion. Black is planning to follow up with b5, creating a pawn storm on the queenside while leaving the center entirely to us for now. This hypermodern approach can be tricky because it lures us into over-extending in the center before Black strikes back with moves like d6 and eventual central breaks. We should respond by continuing our natural development and maintaining our central advantage, but we must stay alert to Black's unusual counterplay ideas that can catch unprepared opponents off guard.
